name: The town of N.
certaintyNote: The unnamed provincial town of N., where Chichikov charms the officials, is Gogol's satirical everytown drawn from the district capitals of Russia; sited in the provinces of the setting.
note: Into the dull provincial town of N. rolls the affable, well-dressed Chichikov, who within days has flattered every official and made himself the toast of local society. Gogol opens on the smug, gossiping bureaucracy of provincial Russia, a world so hollow that a charming nobody with a mad scheme can become its favourite guest.

sources.ref: Dead Souls, Wikipedia, on Chichikov and the town

name: Manilov's estate
certaintyNote: The estate of the sentimental idler Manilov is drawn from the country seats of the provincial gentry; sited near the town of the setting.
note: At the estate of the sugary, do-nothing dreamer Manilov, Chichikov makes his first strange purchase of dead serfs, which his host, too vague to question, hands over as a gift. Gogol begins his gallery of landowners here, each a monstrous distillation of a single vice, Manilov the emptiness of pure sentimental idleness.

name: Sobakevich's estate
certaintyNote: The estate of the bear-like, hard-dealing Sobakevich, and those of the other landowners Chichikov visits, are drawn from provincial Russia; sited in the setting.
note: At the solid, bear-like Sobakevich's, Chichikov meets a shrewd haggler who praises his dead serfs as if selling live ones and drives a hard bargain over men in their graves. Gogol's landowners multiply, the suspicious widow Korobochka, the reckless liar Nozdryov, each a grotesque, the whole tour a diagnosis of a stagnant, serf-owning society.

sources.ref: Dead Souls, Wikipedia, on Sobakevich and the landowners

name: Plyushkin's estate
certaintyNote: The decayed estate of the ultimate miser Plyushkin is drawn from the ruined provincial gentry; sited in the setting.
note: At the rotting estate of Plyushkin, a miser so far gone he lets his wealth and his serfs decay around a hoard of rubbish, Chichikov finds his richest source of dead souls. Gogol reaches the depths of his gallery here, in a portrait of avarice curdled into ruin that is also, unexpectedly, a glimpse of a soul that was once alive and human.

name: The open road
certaintyNote: The endless Russian road along which Chichikov's carriage flees the town once the scheme unravels is a real and recurring feature; sited in the provinces of the setting.
note: When rumour and suspicion finally engulf his scheme, Chichikov's carriage rattles out of town along the open road, and Gogol breaks into his famous rhapsody on Russia herself as a hurtling troika, whither flyest thou, give answer. He ends the surviving book not with a verdict but with that unanswered question, the swindler's flight opening onto a vision of a whole nation racing toward an unknown destiny.

sources.ref: Dead Souls, Wikipedia, on the troika and the ending
